summary: I was born in Burma in 1995, but life there was very difficult. Burmese soldiers destroyed my house and farm, and my family had to live in temporary villages because of the ongoing battles between the Karen people and the Burmese army. Our people were forced off our land and couldn't own property, making life unfair and dangerous.

In 1997, my family fled to Thailand to escape the violence in Burma and lived in a refugee camp there. We stayed in that Thai refugee camp for more than twelve years, relying on support from organizations like the United Nations for food, shelter, and clothing. It was a difficult but necessary refuge before we could start a new life elsewhere.

Thanks to the United Nations' program starting in 2000, my family had the chance to apply to come to the United States in 2007. After passing interviews and background checks, we arrived with the help of the International Organization for Migration. Life in the United States has been wonderful and much easier than in Burma or Thailand, offering us freedom, education, and equal rights.

Currently, I am a student at St. Paul College, which has been an important part of my new life in the United States. Studying there gives me the opportunity to pursue education freely, something I couldn't have in Burma or the refugee camp. It feels good to be able to focus on my future and build a better life for myself and my family.

The United Nations played a crucial role in my family's journey. While we were refugees in Thailand, the UN provided us with food, shelter, and clothing. Later, in 2000, they allowed Karen refugees like us to apply to resettle in the United States, which gave my family hope and eventually led to our new life here.

The International Organization for Migration helped my family the day before we left for the United States by giving us a special bag that was very important for our journey. Their support made it possible for us to travel safely and start our new life in America. I am grateful for their role in helping us transition from refugee life to freedom.

I now live in Minnesota with my family, and I feel safe and hopeful here. Minnesota has become our new home where we can enjoy freedom and equal rights, something we never had before. Being here allows me to focus on my education and remember the struggles my family endured to get to this place.
